findings.title: fetchAll/fetchPendingLeaves/fetchAllLeaves/fetchBalances capture stale permission values due to suppressed exhaustive-deps
findings.description: Three `useEffect` hooks suppress `react-hooks/exhaustive-deps` warnings. The `fetchAll`, `fetchAllLeaves`, and `fetchBalances` functions are defined inline (not wrapped in `useCallback`) and close over `canValidate`, `canReadAllLeaves`, and `canManageBalances` permission values. If permissions change after the initial render (e.g., after an async RBAC load), the effects at lines 227-237 will re-call the stale functions that still hold the old permission booleans. The effect at line 227-225 (`[]` deps) never re-runs at all, so if permissions load asynchronously after mount, `fetchPendingLeaves`, `fetchAllLeaves`, and `fetchBalances` are called with `canValidate=false`, silently skipping data.
findings.root_cause: Inline async helpers are captured by value in effects with suppressed dep warnings, meaning permission-gated fetches use the permission state at first render, not when permissions actually resolve.
findings.impact: If the permission store loads asynchronously (common on hard refresh), validators and admins will see empty 'pending validation' and 'balances' tabs on the initial page load. A manual tab click or full reload is needed to recover. Severity is bounded because the API itself enforces permissions, but UX is degraded and data is silently missing.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) wrap each permission-gated fetch in a `useCallback` with the correct permission deps, so the `fetchAll` useEffect can list them; or (b) trigger a re-fetch when `canValidate`/`canReadAllLeaves`/`canManageBalances` change. The simplest fix: add these booleans to the initial `useEffect` dep array and the individual `selectedUserId`/`balanceYear` effects. Remove the eslint-disable comments after fixing.
acceptance_criteria: Page fetches pending and balance data correctly even when RBAC permissions arrive after the component mounts.
No `eslint-disable react-hooks/exhaustive-deps` comment needed after the fix.
Unit test: mock delayed permission resolution and assert fetchPendingLeaves is called after permissions load.
findings.notes: Three separate suppression comments at lines 224, 231, 236 confirm the issue is known but not fixed.

findings.title: fetchData useCallback in time-tracking/page.tsx misses hasPermission and t dependencies
findings.description: The `fetchData` callback closes over `hasPermission` (from `usePermissions()`) and `t` (translation function) but the dependency array only lists `[user]`. If `hasPermission` returns a new function reference after permissions reload (e.g., after a token refresh), `fetchData` continues to hold the old `hasPermission` reference, meaning the `projects:read` guard uses stale permission data. Similarly `t` is missing but typically stable.
findings.root_cause: The `useCallback` dependency array omits `hasPermission` and `t`, creating a stale closure for permission checks inside the callback.
findings.impact: A user who gains `projects:read` permission in the same session (e.g., role changed, token refreshed) will not see projects in the time-tracking page until a full page reload. Bounded impact but creates confusing UX for admin users.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `hasPermission` and `t` to the `useCallback` dependency array: `}, [user, hasPermission, t]);`. Ensure `usePermissions` hook returns a stable reference (memoized). Alternatively, read `hasPermission` inside the callback via a ref pattern.
acceptance_criteria: No missing dependency ESLint warnings on fetchData in time-tracking/page.tsx.
Project list populates correctly even when permissions are loaded asynchronously.
findings.notes: Same pattern exists in events/page.tsx — see frontend-F3-3.

findings.title: fetchData useCallback in events/page.tsx misses hasPermission dependency
findings.description: The `fetchData` callback in events/page.tsx closes over `hasPermission` (three calls: `projects:read`, `events:readAll`, `events:update`) but the dep array is `[user]`. The same stale-closure risk applies: if RBAC permissions load asynchronously after mount, all three `hasPermission` calls use the stale (empty/initial) permission set, causing projects and user lists to silently not load. The branch `hasPermission('events:readAll')` vs `getByUser(user.id)` is especially sensitive: a wrong branch loads a subset of projects.
findings.root_cause: useCallback dep array omits `hasPermission` which is used for 3 branching permission checks inside the callback.
findings.impact: Users with `events:readAll` who load the page before permissions resolve will see only their own projects in the filter dropdown. Any event linked to another project will appear unfiltered but ungroupable. Confusing for admins.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `hasPermission` to the dependency array: `}, [user, hasPermission]);`. Same guidance as frontend-F3-2.
acceptance_criteria: fetchData re-runs when hasPermission changes reference.
Events page shows correct project scope for all roles after async permission load.

findings.title: usersService.getAll() in telework/page.tsx has no .catch() — silent failure in user selector
findings.description: The `usersService.getAll()` promise has a `.then()` handler but no `.catch()`. If the request fails (network error, 403, 500), the rejection is unhandled. In modern browsers and Next.js, unhandled promise rejections in effects can produce console errors that reach the user-visible error reporter (via `logger.error` if it were called, but it isn't here). More critically, `allUsers` is never populated, so the manager's user-selector dropdown will be empty with no error feedback. The user has no way to know the fetch failed.
findings.root_cause: Missing `.catch()` on a fire-and-forget async operation inside a useEffect.
findings.impact: Managers (telework:manage_any) who encounter a transient API error will see an empty collaborator selector with no error toast. They cannot view or manage other users' telework calendars until they reload. No observability.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `.catch()` handler: `usersService.getAll().then(data => { setAllUsers(Array.isArray(data) ? data : []); }).catch(err => { logger.error('Error fetching users for telework:', err); toast.error(tc('errors.serverError')); });`
acceptance_criteria: When usersService.getAll() rejects, a toast error is shown to the manager.
logger.error is called with the error context.
The selector renders empty with a recoverable state.

findings.title: Leave cancellation request button gated by client-side userId comparison instead of API canRequestCancel flag
findings.code_evidence:
{!showValidationActions &&
              leave.status === LeaveStatus.APPROVED &&
              leave.userId === user?.id && (
                <button
                  onClick={() => handleRequestCancel(leave.id)}
                  className="p-2 text-orange-600 hover:bg-orange-50 rounded-lg transition"
                  title="Demander l'annulation"
                >
                  ↩️
                </button>
              )}
findings.description: The 'request cancellation' button is shown when `leave.status === LeaveStatus.APPROVED && leave.userId === user?.id`. This is a client-side ownership check using the locally stored `user.id` from the auth store (JWT token). Per the CLAUDE.md memory rule, auth decisions must use API-computed flags (canEdit/canDelete per resource). While the API itself should enforce the authorization, the button relies on the frontend correctly reading `user.id` from the JWT, which could be manipulated by a XSS attacker or lead to display inconsistency if a manager is viewing someone else's leave in the all-leaves tab (where `leave.userId !== user.id` may incorrectly hide or show the button). The `canEdit` and `canDelete` flags are correctly API-sourced (lines 757, 767) — this one slip is inconsistent with the established pattern.
findings.root_cause: The requestCancel affordance check was implemented with a direct client-side `userId === user.id` comparison rather than an API-provided `canRequestCancel` flag on the leave object.
findings.impact: Minor inconsistency with the established API-flag pattern. A manager viewing another user's approved leave in the all-leaves tab (showUser=true, showValidationActions=false) will NOT see the button due to userId mismatch — which is correct by accident. The risk is forward: if the all-leaves view ever shows canRequestCancel for delegates, this logic would break. The API enforces the rule, so no actual unauthorized action is possible.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `canRequestCancel` boolean flag to the Leave API response (similar to `canEdit`/`canDelete`). Use that flag: `(leave as Leave & { canRequestCancel?: boolean }).canRequestCancel && leave.status === LeaveStatus.APPROVED`.
acceptance_criteria: Backend returns canRequestCancel: boolean in each Leave object.
Frontend renders the cancel-request button solely based on the API flag.
No client-side userId comparison remains for authorization decisions.
findings.notes: The API enforces the actual permission, so this is primarily an architectural inconsistency rather than an exploitable vulnerability. Severity is 'important' rather than 'blocking'.

findings.title: handleExportExcel fires Promise.all over all active clients without concurrency cap (up to 200 requests)
findings.description: The Excel export function fires one HTTP request per active client simultaneously via `Promise.all`. The list `cap` is bounded by the page-fetch limit of 200 (`getAll({ limit: 200 })`). Sending 200 simultaneous GET requests will saturate the browser's connection pool (typically 6 concurrent per origin), create a thundering-herd on the API, and likely trigger rate-limiting or timeouts. While there is a `> 100` confirm dialog, it only adds friction — the unbounded parallelism is present for any non-zero number of clients.
findings.root_cause: No concurrency limiter (e.g., p-limit, sequential chunking) is applied to the per-client project-summary fetches.
findings.impact: With 100+ active clients, the API may return 429 rate-limit errors or timeout, causing the export to partially fail silently (`.catch()` returns `{ res: null }` for failed items). Users receive an incomplete Excel file without knowing which clients are missing. Server may experience a DoS-like spike during large exports.
findings.suggested_fix: Use a concurrency-limited pattern: import p-limit (already may be in package.json) with limit of 5-10, or fetch in sequential chunks of e.g. 10 clients at a time using a `for...of` loop with `await`. Alternatively, add a backend endpoint that returns all client+project summaries in a single call.
acceptance_criteria: handleExportExcel never fires more than 10 simultaneous requests.
Export completes correctly for 200+ active clients.
Failed per-client fetches show a specific toast indicating partial data.
findings.notes: The `> 100` confirm dialog at line 130 suggests the developer was aware of scale issues but chose UI friction over a proper fix.

findings.title: No React error boundary or Next.js error.tsx in any of the 6 scanned route segments
findings.description: None of the 6 route segments (leaves, telework, time-tracking, clients, third-parties, events) have a co-located `error.tsx` file (Next.js App Router error boundary convention) or a React `<ErrorBoundary>` wrapper in their page component. A runtime rendering error (e.g., null-dereference when API returns unexpected shape, invalid date passed to `new Date()`) in any of these pages will crash the entire route segment and show a white screen with a browser error. The absence of `error.tsx` is confirmed by filesystem search — no such file exists under `apps/web/app/[locale]/` for any of the target directories.
findings.root_cause: No error.tsx or React ErrorBoundary created for these route segments, leaving unhandled render errors as full-page crashes.
findings.impact: Any rendering exception (e.g., `leave.user.department.name` when department is null, or a malformed date string from the API) will crash the entire page with no recovery path. Users see a blank/crash screen instead of a graceful error message. Severity is elevated for pages like leaves and events that are used daily by all staff.
findings.suggested_fix:
Create `apps/web/app/[locale]/leaves/error.tsx` (and similarly for the other 5 directories) following the Next.js App Router pattern: `'use client'; export default function Error({ reset }) { return
...
; }`. Additionally add a shared `` wrapper inside `MainLayout` as a catch-all.
acceptance_criteria: Each of the 6 directories has an error.tsx file.
Navigating to the page after an injected rendering error shows a user-facing error message with a retry button.
The error is logged via logger.error.
findings.notes: This finding covers all 6 route segments under the shard. The evidence file listed is leaves/page.tsx as representative.

findings.title: All 6 pages use manual fetch + useState with no TanStack Query caching — repeated fetches on every navigation
findings.description: All 6 pages use the same pattern: `useEffect + manual fetch + useState`. None use TanStack Query which is listed in the project stack (CLAUDE.md). Every navigation to these pages triggers a full re-fetch (no staleTime, no background revalidation, no deduplication). The leaves page fires 8 concurrent requests on every mount. The CLAUDE.md convention specifies TanStack Query for frontend data fetching.
findings.root_cause: Pages were built without adopting TanStack Query, leading to manual fetch orchestration with no caching layer.
findings.impact: Unnecessary network load on every navigation; no optimistic updates possible without rollback logic; no deduplication when multiple components fetch the same data. Navigation feels slower than needed. This is nit severity as it's a design debt issue rather than a bug.
findings.suggested_fix: Migrate to `useQuery` / `useMutation` from TanStack Query. Define query keys per domain (e.g., `['leaves', 'mine']`, `['leaves', 'pending']`). Set `staleTime: 30_000` for relatively static data like leave types and balances. Use `invalidateQueries` after mutations instead of calling `fetchAll()`.
acceptance_criteria: Each page uses useQuery for data fetching.
Navigating away and back does not trigger a loading spinner if data is fresh.
Mutations use useMutation and invalidate relevant query keys.
findings.notes: Nit because it is a well-known architectural debt that requires significant refactoring. Not a security or correctness risk, but impacts UX performance noticeably.

verified_clean: No dangerouslySetInnerHTML usage in any of the 8 scanned files
No secrets, tokens, or credentials exposed to the client bundle in any scanned file
No console.* calls with PII — all files use the centralized logger with PII scrubbing (logger.ts)
No hardcoded role constants (Role.ADMIN, Role.MANAGER) in client-side auth decisions — all RBAC checks use hasPermission() from usePermissions hook
No open redirect vulnerabilities — all router.push() calls use hardcoded locale-prefixed paths

No XSS via CSS injection — leaveType.color from API is used directly in style prop (backgroundColor, color) without HTML interpolation; CSS property value injection has no code-execution path in React
Blob URL for template download (leaves/page.tsx:524) correctly calls URL.revokeObjectURL() immediately after click
mailto: href in third-parties/[id]/page.tsx uses server-sourced contactEmail — no open redirect to external HTTP URLs
canEdit and canDelete flags in leaves/page.tsx (lines 757, 767) correctly use API-returned flags from the leave object, not client-side computation
telework/page.tsx fetchTeleworkData useCallback has correct deps [currentMonth, selectedUserId, tc]
clients/page.tsx and third-parties/page.tsx permission checks (canCreate, canUpdate, canDelete) correctly use hasPermission() hook
clients/[id]/page.tsx permission checks correctly use hasPermission() hook
third-parties/[id]/page.tsx permission checks correctly use hasPermission() hook
events/page.tsx canCreateEvent() and canDeleteEvent() correctly use hasPermission() hook
Test files exist for leaves (TST-023) and telework covering RBAC affordances
No localStorage or sessionStorage access for tokens in any scanned file
All pages use 'use client' directive correctly — no attempted server-side secret access
